Self-paced legal support training for paralegal and BPO workflows.

U.S. Bankruptcy Law and Practice

A detailed interactive course book for learners who need bankruptcy knowledge they can connect to real paralegal and business process outsourcing workflows.

Start Chapter 1 See Workflow Map Statewise Explorer
19chapters
95quiz questions
19practice scenarios
Offlinesingle-file book

How This Book Teaches

Each chapter gives you the legal idea first, then shows how that idea becomes a real support task: intake, document review, petition drafting, docketing, claims review, quality control, or attorney escalation.

1

Read simply. Start in Guided mode if you want the plain-language path.

2

Go deeper. Switch to Deep mode for primary sources, terms, and full context.

3

Practice. Use Practice mode for scenarios, workflows, red flags, assignments, notes, and quizzes.

4

Think like support staff. Every legal issue becomes a fact to collect, a document to verify, a deadline to track, or an attorney-review note.

Role Boundary

This course trains supervised legal support work. Learners cannot give legal advice, select chapters, choose exemptions, predict discharge, or decide strategy. The professional habit is simple: collect facts, organize documents, draft carefully, and route legal judgment to attorney review.

Review and Use Notices

These notes keep the course clean for review and help prevent the material from being used beyond its training purpose.

Not Legal AdviceThis is educational content for supervised bankruptcy support training. It does not authorize legal advice or legal representation.
Verify Before UseBankruptcy forms, dollar amounts, means-test data, local rules, and state exemptions change. Live work needs current authority and attorney instruction.
IPR HygieneThe book is generated original training text with an embedded generated bitmap background. It links to official public sources instead of copying protected manuals or commercial course material.

Official Source Links

These are the authority anchors. Learners can use them while studying and verify current versions before live production work.

U.S. Courts Bankruptcy BasicsU.S. Courts Bankruptcy ProcessAbout U.S. Bankruptcy CourtsU.S. Courts Court Website LinksU.S. Courts Chapter 7 BasicsU.S. Courts Chapter 11 BasicsU.S. Courts Chapter 13 BasicsOfficial Bankruptcy FormsFederal Rules of Bankruptcy ProcedureTitle 11, United States Code11 U.S.C. 522, ExemptionsBankruptcy Rule 4003, ExemptionsFederal Register 2025 Bankruptcy Dollar AdjustmentsU.S. Trustee ProgramU.S. Trustee Means TestingCredit Counseling and Debtor Education

Practical Terminology Coach

Plain-English meanings, practice examples, and memory hooks for the words learners see again and again.

Bankruptcy CodeOpen example

The main federal bankruptcy law in Title 11.

Practice example
When the lesson says section 362, it means section 362 of Title 11, the automatic stay section.
Think: the rulebook.
Bankruptcy RulesOpen example

The national procedure rules for bankruptcy cases.

Practice example
A filing deadline or motion procedure often comes from the Federal Rules of Bankruptcy Procedure.
Think: the process map.
Local RulesOpen example

Court-specific rules for a particular bankruptcy court.

Practice example
A Chapter 13 plan form may be required in one district but different in another.
Think: the local house rules.
DebtorOpen example

The person or business in bankruptcy.

Practice example
A wage earner filing Chapter 13 is the debtor.
Think: the case starts with this person or entity.
CreditorOpen example

Someone claiming the debtor owes money or property rights.

Practice example
A credit card bank, mortgage lender, landlord, or medical provider can be a creditor.
Think: the party asking to be paid.
TrusteeOpen example

A bankruptcy professional assigned to administer or monitor the case.

Practice example
A Chapter 7 trustee reviews assets; a Chapter 13 trustee monitors plan payments.
Think: the case monitor.
Automatic StayOpen example

A legal pause on many collection actions after filing.

Practice example
A wage garnishment may need urgent attorney review after a case is filed.
Think: the stop sign, with exceptions.
Bankruptcy EstateOpen example

The legal pool of property interests created when the case is filed.

Practice example
A tax refund, vehicle, bank account, or lawsuit claim may be estate property.
Think: what comes into the case.
ExemptionOpen example

A law that may protect certain property.

Practice example
A homestead, vehicle, household goods, or retirement exemption may be reviewed.
Think: possible protection, attorney decides.
Means TestOpen example

A bankruptcy income-and-expense calculation used in many consumer cases.

Practice example
The team collects pay stubs and state median income data; the attorney reviews the result.
Think: income screening.
ScheduleOpen example

A bankruptcy form section that discloses assets, debts, income, expenses, or related facts.

Practice example
Schedule A/B lists property; Schedule I lists income; Schedule J lists expenses.
Think: organized disclosure.
SOFAOpen example

Statement of Financial Affairs, a form about financial history.

Practice example
It asks about income, lawsuits, transfers, losses, payments, and business history.
Think: financial backstory.
Proof of ClaimOpen example

A creditor's filed request for payment in the bankruptcy case.

Practice example
A mortgage lender files a claim showing arrears and loan balance.
Think: creditor's bill to the case.
Priority DebtOpen example

A debt category that may receive special payment treatment.

Practice example
Certain taxes or domestic support obligations can be priority claims.
Think: paid attention first.
Secured DebtOpen example

Debt tied to collateral.

Practice example
A car loan is usually secured by the vehicle.
Think: debt with property attached.
DischargeOpen example

A court order affecting personal liability for dischargeable debts.

Practice example
A discharge does not automatically remove every lien or every debt category.
Think: personal liability relief, not magic eraser.
ReaffirmationOpen example

An agreement that may keep personal liability on a debt after bankruptcy.

Practice example
A debtor keeping a car may receive reaffirmation papers for attorney review.
Think: post-bankruptcy promise, high caution.
DocketOpen example

The official case activity record.

Practice example
A motion, order, notice, claim, or discharge appears on the docket.
Think: case timeline.
DomicileOpen example

A residence-related legal concept that can affect exemption law.

Practice example
A debtor who moved states recently creates an exemption review issue.
Think: which state's protection rules might apply.
Red FlagOpen example

A fact that needs attention before routine processing continues.

Practice example
Recent transfer to a relative, foreclosure sale date, or missing tax return.
Think: pause and escalate.

Federal Exemption Snapshot

Selected federal bankruptcy exemption and cap amounts from the April 1, 2025 adjustment. These are training anchors only. Learners need current amounts, state opt-out rules, domicile facts, and attorney instructions verified before live Schedule C work.

Homestead under 11 U.S.C. 522(d)(1)$31,575
Motor vehicle under 522(d)(2)$5,025
Household goods aggregate under 522(d)(3)$16,850, with per-item cap
Jewelry under 522(d)(4)$2,125
Wildcard under 522(d)(5)$1,675 plus up to $15,800 of unused homestead
Tools of trade under 522(d)(6)$3,175
Personal injury payment under 522(d)(11)(D)$31,575
Certain retirement funds cap under 522(n)$1,711,975
State homestead acquisition cap under 522(p)/(q)$214,000

Chapter 1

What Bankruptcy Is and How a Case Moves

5 to 6 hours

Learning Map

Before reading, use this map to see what the chapter helps you do in practice.

  • Explain bankruptcy as a federal legal process, not a private debt negotiation.
  • Identify the main participants in a bankruptcy case and what each one does.
  • Describe the ordinary life cycle of a consumer bankruptcy case.
  • Separate legal advice from supervised support work.

Read the Lesson

Bankruptcy is a federal court process that deals with debt, property, creditor rights, and debtor relief in one organized system. A person or business may be overwhelmed by collection pressure from many directions: lawsuits, garnishments, repossessions, foreclosure, tax notices, medical bills, credit cards, vendor claims, or business lease defaults. Bankruptcy does not simply mean that debts disappear. It means the debtor enters a court-supervised process where assets, debts, income, expenses, creditor rights, and legal protections are disclosed and handled under federal law.

The first idea to learn is that bankruptcy is both protective and collective. It is protective because the filing of a case can create an automatic stay that generally pauses many collection actions. It is collective because creditors are brought into one court process instead of racing against each other. The bankruptcy system asks: What does the debtor own? What does the debtor owe? What law protects property? Which creditors have collateral? Which claims have priority? What can be paid? What can be discharged? Which disputes need a judge?

The main parties are the debtor, creditors, trustee, judge, U.S. Trustee, attorneys, and sometimes committees or examiners in larger business cases. The debtor is the person or entity seeking relief or reorganizing debt. Creditors are people or entities asserting a right to payment or property. The trustee performs duties that vary by chapter: a Chapter 7 trustee may administer estate assets, while a Chapter 13 trustee receives plan payments and monitors compliance. The bankruptcy judge resolves disputes and enters orders. The U.S. Trustee Program supervises the integrity of the system in many districts.

For paralegal and BPO work, the most important practical skill is understanding where a task fits in the case life cycle. A case may begin with intake, document collection, credit counseling, petition preparation, attorney review, signing, filing, notice, trustee review, meeting of creditors, objections or motions, discharge, dismissal, conversion, plan confirmation, or closing. Each stage creates different documents and deadlines. Good support staff do not treat bankruptcy as random paperwork. They see each document as part of a procedural chain.

A second essential skill is role discipline. A paralegal or BPO analyst may collect facts, organize documents, prepare drafts, enter data into software, review dockets, summarize notices, prepare checklists, and flag issues for attorney review. They may not tell a debtor which chapter to file, whether property is exempt, whether a debt will be discharged, whether a creditor is violating the stay, or what legal strategy to choose. The safe phrase is: I will flag this for attorney review.

The chapters of bankruptcy are different tracks. Chapter 7 is often called liquidation, although many consumer Chapter 7 cases have no nonexempt assets to distribute. Chapter 13 is a repayment plan for eligible individuals with regular income. Chapter 11 is reorganization, commonly used by businesses and sometimes individuals with more complex debt. Subchapter V is a streamlined Chapter 11 path for qualifying small business debtors. A support professional can know the basic purpose of each chapter, but chapter selection is attorney work.

Key Terms

Click a term card and explain it out loud before checking yourself.

Primary Source Focus

These are the official-law reading anchors for the chapter.

  • U.S. Courts Bankruptcy Basics.
  • Title 11 overview: scan chapters 1, 3, 5, 7, 11, and 13.
  • Official Voluntary Petition form.

Practice Scenario

New Consumer Intake

Facts: Maria receives a wage garnishment notice, has $42,000 in credit card debt, owns a car with a loan, rents an apartment, and has not filed bankruptcy before. She asks the intake team whether Chapter 7 will remove all her debts.

The support team can collect facts and documents, but avoid answering whether Chapter 7 is best or whether all debts will be removed. A proper note would say: Client reports active wage garnishment, credit card debt, vehicle loan, no prior bankruptcy known, and asks chapter/discharge question. Please review chapter options, stay timing, and discharge issues.

Workflow: What You Do in Practice

1

Open a case file and record debtor name, contact information, urgent collection facts, prior bankruptcy history if known, and document status.

2

Create a missing document list and send only approved administrative requests.

3

Prepare a case timeline with intake date, expected filing target, credit counseling status, and urgent creditor action.

4

Flag any client question that asks what the client can do legally.

Red Flags to Escalate

  • Foreclosure sale, repossession, eviction, garnishment, levy, utility shutoff, or lawsuit deadline.
  • Prior bankruptcy cases, especially recent cases.
  • Unusual asset transfers, cash withdrawals, insider repayments, or hidden business interests.
  • Client asks for legal advice directly.

Practice Assignment

Use these as workbook tasks. A trainer or attorney reviewer can grade them using the capstone rubric.

  • Draw the life cycle of a Chapter 7 case and a Chapter 13 case on one page.
  • Write a safe intake note for a client who asks: can I file now or wait until after payday?
  • Make a vocabulary list of 25 bankruptcy terms from this chapter and define them in your own words.

Knowledge Check

Choose an answer. The book gives immediate feedback and tracks your score inside this browser.

1. Bankruptcy is mainly handled in which court system?

2. Who may give legal advice to a debtor?

3. Which chapter is usually associated with an individual repayment plan?

4. What can support staff do with an urgent foreclosure date?

5. What is the best description of support work?

My Notes

Chapter 2

Reading Bankruptcy Law Like a Legal Professional

6 hours

Learning Map

Before reading, use this map to see what the chapter helps you do in practice.

  • Identify the major sources used in bankruptcy practice.
  • Read a statute by breaking it into elements, definitions, exceptions, and consequences.
  • Use IRAC to organize a support memo without giving legal advice.
  • Understand why local rules and judge procedures matter.

Read the Lesson

Bankruptcy work is source-driven. The most important sources are Title 11 of the United States Code, the Federal Rules of Bankruptcy Procedure, Official Bankruptcy Forms, local bankruptcy court rules, judge-specific procedures, court orders, and attorney instructions. A learner can stop thinking of bankruptcy as a set of memorized answers and start thinking of it as a system of authorities. When a question arises, the professional habit is to ask: Which source controls this task?

Title 11 contains the Bankruptcy Code. It includes definitions, eligibility rules, estate rules, automatic stay provisions, creditor claim provisions, discharge rules, and chapter-specific rules. The Bankruptcy Rules explain procedure: filing, notice, claims, motions, adversary proceedings, privacy, and time computation. Official Forms translate legal requirements into standardized filing documents. Local rules add district-specific requirements. A judge's order may control a specific case even if a general checklist says something else.

To read a statute, begin with the triggering condition. Ask who the rule applies to, what event activates it, what needs to be shown, what exceptions exist, what deadline applies, and what consequence follows. Defined terms are essential. A word such as claim, creditor, debt, entity, or transfer may have a specific statutory meaning that is broader than everyday speech. Cross-references are also essential because one section may only make sense when read with another.

JD-style learning uses case briefing and IRAC. A case brief identifies facts, issue, rule, reasoning, holding, and practical significance. IRAC means Issue, Rule, Application, Conclusion. A paralegal or BPO worker avoid write legal conclusions for a client, but can use a modified IRAC for internal notes. The issue might be: possible recent insider transfer. The rule source might be sections on estate property or avoidance. The application is a factual summary from documents. The conclusion is best handled as framed as: attorney review needed.

Local rules are not optional. Bankruptcy courts may require specific forms, negative notice language, hearing procedures, plan forms, certificate language, and service methods. A national training course can teach the framework, but live production needs to check the relevant court. A support worker who prepares a perfect national form but misses a local rule can still create a serious filing problem.

Legal reading also requires humility. If a statute contains exceptions, if a rule depends on timing, if a form instruction is unclear, or if the facts are incomplete, the answer is not to guess. The answer is to preserve the question and escalate it. In good legal operations, uncertainty is not a failure. Hidden uncertainty is the failure.

Key Terms

Click a term card and explain it out loud before checking yourself.

Primary Source Focus

These are the official-law reading anchors for the chapter.

  • 11 U.S.C. secs. 101, 109, 301, 302, 341, 362, 521, 522, 541.
  • Bankruptcy Rules 1007, 2002, 3001, 4001, 4004, 5005, 7001, 9011, 9037.
  • Official Bankruptcy Forms page.

Practice Scenario

Using Modified IRAC

Facts: The debtor's bank statements show a $5,000 payment to the debtor's brother two months before filing. The questionnaire says it was repayment of a family loan.

Issue: possible insider payment before filing. Rule/source: preference and transfer review may be relevant; attorney to determine. Application: bank statement dated within two months shows $5,000 to brother; client describes loan repayment. Conclusion for support memo: attorney review required; request loan documents, payment history, and relationship confirmation.

Workflow: What You Do in Practice

1

Attach source references to internal notes when possible.

2

Compare task instructions with current forms and local rules.

3

Use issue labels such as timing issue, exemption issue, claim issue, discharge issue, or stay issue.

4

Keep legal questions separate from clerical corrections.

Red Flags to Escalate

  • Template instructions that do not match current official forms.
  • Local rule requirements not captured in the national checklist.
  • Legal words used casually without checking definitions.
  • A deadline calculated from the wrong triggering event.

Practice Assignment

Use these as workbook tasks. A trainer or attorney reviewer can grade them using the capstone rubric.

  • Read 11 U.S.C. sec. 362 and list five things it may stop and three things that require attorney review.
  • Brief one bankruptcy case selected by the trainer using facts, issue, rule, holding, and practice takeaway.
  • Create a source hierarchy chart for your future bankruptcy work.

Knowledge Check

Choose an answer. The book gives immediate feedback and tracks your score inside this browser.

1. Where is the Bankruptcy Code primarily found?

2. What does the R in IRAC stand for?

3. Why do definitions matter?

4. What can staff do if local rules conflict with an old checklist?

5. What is a safe internal conclusion?

My Notes

Chapter 3

Eligibility, Chapters, and Starting the Case

6 hours

Learning Map

Before reading, use this map to see what the chapter helps you do in practice.

  • Distinguish Chapter 7, Chapter 11, Chapter 13, and Subchapter V at a practical level.
  • Understand basic eligibility concepts without making eligibility decisions.
  • Identify voluntary, joint, and involuntary case concepts.
  • Track credit counseling and debtor education requirements.

Read the Lesson

Bankruptcy cases do not all begin the same way or serve the same purpose. The chapter selected controls the case structure. Chapter 7 usually focuses on disclosure, trustee review, possible liquidation of nonexempt assets, and discharge. Chapter 13 focuses on a repayment plan by an individual with regular income. Chapter 11 focuses on reorganization or structured liquidation, often for a business. Subchapter V is a special small business reorganization path within Chapter 11. A support professional needs to understand these differences because they affect documents, deadlines, trustee requests, and work queues.

Eligibility is a legal question. However, support staff needs to collect the facts that allow an attorney to analyze eligibility. Important facts include debtor type, income source, household size, debts, prior bankruptcy filings, business activity, credit counseling status, assets, and urgent creditor action. Chapter 13, for example, requires an individual with regular income and is affected by debt limits under current law. Chapter 7 consumer cases may require means test review. Chapter 11 may involve business operations, corporate authority, and U.S. Trustee reporting.

Most bankruptcy cases begin with a voluntary petition filed by the debtor. A married couple may file a joint case. In limited circumstances, creditors may file an involuntary petition against a debtor. The petition is not merely a cover sheet. It is the document that starts the case, creates the case number, identifies the debtor, identifies the chapter, and triggers major legal consequences. Errors in debtor identity, chapter, venue, or filing status can be serious.

Individual debtors commonly needs to complete approved credit counseling before filing unless an exception applies. They often needs to complete debtor education after filing and before discharge. Support teams can track provider name, completion date, certificate status, and filing status. They avoid deciding whether an exception applies. A missing or stale certificate is best handled as escalated immediately.

Corporate and business debtors add another layer. A corporation, LLC, or partnership may need evidence of authority to file, such as a resolution or consent. Business cases may require ownership information, tax documents, payroll information, leases, secured debt schedules, insurance, bank accounts, and vendor lists. BPO teams handling business bankruptcy need stronger document indexing because the file can become large quickly.

The central support habit in this module is neutral screening. A screening form avoid say: debtor qualifies for Chapter 7. It can say: facts collected for attorney review. That protects the learner, the firm, and the client.

Key Terms

Click a term card and explain it out loud before checking yourself.

Primary Source Focus

These are the official-law reading anchors for the chapter.

  • 11 U.S.C. secs. 109, 301, 302, 303.
  • Official Form B101 Voluntary Petition.
  • U.S. Trustee credit counseling and debtor education materials.

Practice Scenario

Chapter Screening Without Legal Advice

Facts: A self-employed debtor has high credit card debt, two vehicles, unpaid payroll taxes from a closed business, and one prior Chapter 7 discharge six years ago.

The support note avoid choosing a chapter. It can identify facts for review: self-employment, business tax debt, vehicle collateral, prior Chapter 7 discharge six years ago, need for income proof, tax records, business closure documents, and attorney review of eligibility/discharge timing.

Workflow: What You Do in Practice

1

Collect prior bankruptcy case numbers, filing dates, chapter, discharge/dismissal status, and district if known.

2

Track credit counseling and debtor education certificates separately.

3

Confirm debtor type and any business ownership.

4

Create a chapter comparison worksheet for internal training, not for client advice.

Red Flags to Escalate

  • Recent prior filings or prior discharge.
  • Missing credit counseling certificate close to planned filing.
  • Entity debtor without corporate authority documents.
  • Client wants to file only to delay an action but has not provided full facts.

Practice Assignment

Use these as workbook tasks. A trainer or attorney reviewer can grade them using the capstone rubric.

  • Prepare a neutral chapter screening intake form that collects facts but gives no recommendations.
  • Compare Chapter 7, 13, and 11 in a one-page table.
  • Draft an escalation note for missing credit counseling on the day before a foreclosure sale.

Knowledge Check

Choose an answer. The book gives immediate feedback and tracks your score inside this browser.

1. What starts most voluntary bankruptcy cases?

2. Who decides whether an exception to credit counseling applies?

3. A joint case is filed by whom?

4. Chapter 11 is commonly used for what?

5. What is neutral screening?

My Notes

Chapter 4

Property of the Estate, Schedules, and Exemptions

7 hours

Learning Map

Before reading, use this map to see what the chapter helps you do in practice.

  • Explain the bankruptcy estate in practical language.
  • Connect assets to Schedule A/B and exemptions to Schedule C.
  • Identify valuation, ownership, lien, and transfer issues.
  • Prepare an asset review log for attorney review.

Read the Lesson

When a bankruptcy case is filed, a bankruptcy estate is created. The estate is a legal concept, not a physical box. It generally includes broad interests of the debtor in property as of the filing date, subject to statutory rules and exceptions. The estate may include real estate, vehicles, bank accounts, cash, household goods, electronics, jewelry, business interests, tax refunds, legal claims, insurance rights, inheritances in certain time periods, and other rights that have value. Support staff can assume disclosure is broad and allow the attorney to decide legal treatment.

Schedule A/B is the main property schedule for individual debtors. It asks for real property and personal property. A support worker avoid treat small assets as unimportant simply because the client thinks they have little value. Disclosure is different from exemption. A debtor may need to disclose property even if it is fully protected or has little resale value. Omissions can damage credibility and create legal problems.

Schedule C is where exemptions are claimed. Exemptions protect certain property from being administered for creditors, but the rules vary by state, federal option, domicile, asset type, and amount. Selecting exemptions is legal work. A paralegal may draft entries using attorney-approved instructions and jurisdictional templates, but avoid deciding exemption strategy alone.

Asset work is source work. A home value might come from a comparative market analysis, appraisal, tax assessment, online estimate, or client estimate, depending on attorney policy. A car value might come from a valuation guide, dealer quote, loan statement, or client estimate. Bank balances is best handled as tied to statements close to filing. Retirement accounts need statements and account type. Business interests need ownership documents and financial information. Legal claims need pleadings, demand letters, accident reports, or attorney correspondence.

Ownership matters. Property may be owned by the debtor alone, jointly with a spouse, jointly with a non-filing person, by a business entity, in trust, or held for someone else. The name on title may differ from who uses the property. A support professional can document title, possession, payments, and the client's explanation, then flag inconsistencies.

Transfers are just as important as current assets. A debtor may have sold a car, repaid a family member, transferred title to a relative, closed an account, gifted property, or moved business assets before filing. These facts can raise avoidance, exemption, disclosure, or good-faith issues. The support role is to build a timeline and preserve documents.

Key Terms

Click a term card and explain it out loud before checking yourself.

Primary Source Focus

These are the official-law reading anchors for the chapter.

  • 11 U.S.C. secs. 541, 542, 522, 554.
  • Official Forms B106A/B and B106C.
  • Applicable state or federal exemption references selected by attorney.

Practice Scenario

Asset Disclosure Problem

Facts: The debtor says she owns no valuable property. Bank statements show payments for a storage unit. The questionnaire lists no stored items.

Support staff avoid assuming the storage is empty. The issue log can request details about the storage unit, contents, ownership, estimated value, photos or inventory if required by attorney policy, and payment source. The attorney decides disclosure and exemption treatment.

Workflow: What You Do in Practice

1

Create an asset log with description, owner, location, value, value source, lien, document source, and review notes.

2

Compare bank statements, insurance records, tax returns, and questionnaires for hidden or omitted assets.

3

Track asset transfers by date, transferee, relationship, amount/value, and document support.

4

Keep exemption questions separate from asset disclosure facts.

Red Flags to Escalate

  • Property titled in someone else's name but paid for or used by the debtor.
  • Recent title changes, gifts, sales, or repayments.
  • Crypto accounts, payment apps, online stores, side businesses, or pending lawsuits.
  • Large tax refund expected but not listed.

Practice Assignment

Use these as workbook tasks. A trainer or attorney reviewer can grade them using the capstone rubric.

  • Build an asset review worksheet for a fictional debtor with a home, car, retirement account, side business, tax refund, and pending injury claim.
  • Draft a missing information request for asset values without suggesting exemption outcomes.
  • Identify ten places in ordinary documents where hidden assets may appear.

Knowledge Check

Choose an answer. The book gives immediate feedback and tracks your score inside this browser.

1. Which schedule lists many property interests?

2. Who can choose exemptions?

3. Why are transfers important?

4. What can support staff do when asset ownership is unclear?

5. Disclosure and exemption are the same thing.

My Notes

Chapter 5

The Automatic Stay and Urgent Filing Work

6 hours

Learning Map

Before reading, use this map to see what the chapter helps you do in practice.

  • Explain the automatic stay and why it is central to bankruptcy practice.
  • Identify urgent collection events that require immediate escalation.
  • Understand that the stay has exceptions and repeat-filing limits.
  • Prepare emergency filing support without promising legal outcomes.

Read the Lesson

The automatic stay is one of the most powerful features of bankruptcy. When a case is filed, the stay generally stops many actions to collect prepetition debts, enforce liens, continue lawsuits, garnish wages, repossess property, conduct foreclosure sales, or contact the debtor for payment. The stay gives the debtor breathing room and protects the bankruptcy court's control over the case. For support staff, stay-related work is often urgent because timing can decide whether a sale, garnishment, or repossession proceeds.

The stay is automatic in the sense that it arises by operation of law when the case is filed, but it is not simple. It has exceptions. It may be limited or may not arise in the same way when there are prior cases. Certain family law, criminal, tax, eviction, or regulatory matters may be treated differently. Creditors may file motions for relief from stay. A paralegal or BPO analyst can never promise that a specific creditor action will stop. The correct support response is to collect the facts and escalate quickly.

Urgent stay work begins with facts. For foreclosure, the team needs sale date, property address, mortgage creditor, loan number, foreclosure case number, notices, reinstatement figures if available, and attorney instructions. For repossession, the team needs vehicle location, repossession status, creditor, collateral description, and whether the vehicle has already been sold. For garnishment, the team needs employer, court case, creditor, pay date, and garnishment order. For lawsuits, the team needs hearing dates, answer deadlines, and case caption.

After filing, notice matters. The court will issue notice, but urgent situations may require attorney-approved direct notice to creditors, foreclosure counsel, sheriff, employer, or repossession agent. Staff needs to follow office procedures and avoid giving legal opinions. A notice email avoid say: You are violating the stay and will be sanctioned. Unless approved by counsel, it can simply transmit filing information or documents according to attorney instruction.

Relief from stay motions are common in secured debt cases. A mortgage lender may seek permission to proceed with foreclosure if payments are not being made or there is no equity. A vehicle lender may seek relief if insurance or payments are missing. Support staff can extract the hearing date, objection deadline, collateral, movant, loan number, arrears alleged, declaration exhibits, and service list. The attorney decides the response.

The best stay workflow is calm and fast. Panic creates errors. A good emergency checklist prevents missing the exact fact that matters most, such as the sale time, case number, creditor counsel, or prior filing history.

Key Terms

Click a term card and explain it out loud before checking yourself.

Primary Source Focus

These are the official-law reading anchors for the chapter.

  • 11 U.S.C. secs. 362 and 1301.
  • Bankruptcy Rule 4001.
  • Local emergency filing procedures.

Practice Scenario

Foreclosure Tomorrow

Facts: A debtor calls at 3:00 p.m. and says her home foreclosure sale is tomorrow at 10:00 a.m. She filed a Chapter 13 case last year that was dismissed.

This is urgent and legally sensitive. Support staff can immediately collect foreclosure notice, property address, mortgage creditor, sale time, prior case number, dismissal date, credit counseling status, and all attorney-required filing documents. The note needs to flag prior filing and stay review. Staff avoid promise that filing will stop the sale.

Workflow: What You Do in Practice

1

Maintain emergency intake checklists for foreclosure, repossession, garnishment, eviction, utility shutoff, and lawsuits.

2

Record exact dates and times, not vague descriptions like soon or next week.

3

After filing, distribute filing information only under attorney-approved procedures.

4

Calendar stay relief response deadlines and hearings immediately.

Red Flags to Escalate

  • Any sale, hearing, repossession, or garnishment within seven days.
  • Prior bankruptcy case within the last year.
  • Creditor action after filing.
  • Client asks whether a creditor will be punished.

Practice Assignment

Use these as workbook tasks. A trainer or attorney reviewer can grade them using the capstone rubric.

  • Create an emergency foreclosure intake checklist.
  • Summarize a sample motion for relief from stay in one page.
  • Write a safe internal note for a client asking: If I file today, can the lender still sell my house?

Knowledge Check

Choose an answer. The book gives immediate feedback and tracks your score inside this browser.

1. What does the automatic stay generally do?

2. Can support staff promise that a foreclosure will stop?

3. What is a relief from stay motion?

4. Which fact is critical in emergency foreclosure work?

5. Prior filings may affect the stay.

My Notes

Chapter 6

Creditors, Claims, Secured Debt, and Priority

7 hours

Learning Map

Before reading, use this map to see what the chapter helps you do in practice.

  • Classify creditor information for schedules and claims review.
  • Distinguish secured, priority unsecured, and general unsecured debts.
  • Understand the proof of claim process.
  • Track claims deadlines and objections.

Read the Lesson

Bankruptcy requires accurate creditor information because notice and payment depend on it. A creditor may have a secured claim, priority unsecured claim, general unsecured claim, administrative claim, domestic support claim, tax claim, lease claim, or other special status. The classification affects schedules, plans, distributions, objections, and discharge analysis. Staff do not make final legal classifications independently, but they needs to organize the facts so the attorney can review them efficiently.

A secured creditor has an interest in collateral. Common secured debts include mortgages, vehicle loans, tax liens, judgment liens, equipment loans, and purchase-money security interests. A creditor statement may show the balance, payment amount, interest rate, arrears, collateral, escrow, fees, and address for notices. The team can connect each secured debt to the relevant asset. If a home is listed, there is best handled as review of any mortgage. If a car loan is listed, the car can appear on Schedule A/B.

Priority unsecured debts receive special treatment under the Bankruptcy Code. Examples may include certain domestic support obligations, taxes, wages, and other categories. General unsecured debts often include credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, deficiency balances, and old utilities. Some debts require discharge review even if they are unsecured, such as student loans, fraud allegations, fines, restitution, and support obligations.

The creditor matrix is a notice tool. It lists creditor names and addresses for case notices. Bad addresses can create serious problems. A support worker can use reliable sources: statements, collection letters, credit reports, lawsuits, proofs of claim, and client information. When multiple addresses exist, office policy and attorney instruction control which addresses are used.

A proof of claim is a creditor's filing asserting a right to payment from the estate or plan. In Chapter 13, proofs of claim are central to plan administration. A claim may be secured, priority, or unsecured. It may include attachments, payment history, mortgage arrears, escrow information, interest, fees, or tax details. Staff can compare claims to schedules, identify duplicates, track claim numbers, and flag objections for attorney review.

Claims work demands deadline discipline. A bar date is a deadline for filing claims or other actions. Governmental claim deadlines may differ from non-government deadlines. Claims may be amended, transferred, withdrawn, objected to, or disallowed. A BPO team that supports claims review can maintain a claims register worksheet and update it whenever the docket changes.

Key Terms

Click a term card and explain it out loud before checking yourself.

Primary Source Focus

These are the official-law reading anchors for the chapter.

  • 11 U.S.C. secs. 501, 502, 503, 506, 507.
  • Bankruptcy Rules 3001, 3002, 3002.1, 3007.
  • Official Form B410 Proof of Claim.

Practice Scenario

Conflicting Creditor Information

Facts: The credit report lists a debt owed to ABC Bank. A collection letter lists XYZ Recovery for the same account number. The lawsuit caption lists XYZ Recovery as assignee of ABC Bank.

The support worksheet can list all known names, source documents, account number tail, lawsuit status, and address options. It can flag possible duplicate/assignee issue for attorney review and matrix instructions.

Workflow: What You Do in Practice

1

Create a creditor spreadsheet with name, address, debt type, account tail, balance, collateral, source, schedule target, and notes.

2

Compare credit report accounts to lawsuits, collection letters, and client questionnaires.

3

Track proof of claim number, filed date, creditor, amount, classification, amendments, and objection status.

4

Escalate duplicates, disputed debts, liens, tax debts, student loans, and support obligations.

Red Flags to Escalate

  • Creditor address missing or returned mail.
  • Same debt appears under multiple names.
  • Collateral debt with no matching asset.
  • Mortgage claim includes unexpected arrears or fees.

Practice Assignment

Use these as workbook tasks. A trainer or attorney reviewer can grade them using the capstone rubric.

  • Prepare a creditor classification worksheet from ten sample debts.
  • Review a sample proof of claim and identify the amount, classification, attachments, and objections to flag.
  • Create a returned-mail escalation SOP.

Knowledge Check

Choose an answer. The book gives immediate feedback and tracks your score inside this browser.

1. A mortgage lender usually has what type of claim?

2. What is the purpose of the creditor matrix?

3. Who decides whether to object to a claim?

4. Which rule is important for Chapter 13 mortgage notices?

5. A bar date is a deadline.

My Notes

Chapter 7

Income, Expenses, Means Testing, and Financial Schedules

7 hours

Learning Map

Before reading, use this map to see what the chapter helps you do in practice.

  • Understand why income and expense accuracy matters.
  • Connect pay records, tax returns, bank statements, and business records to schedules.
  • Explain the means test as a data collection and attorney review process.
  • Identify inconsistencies that require follow-up.

Read the Lesson

Income and expense information affects Chapter 7 means testing, Chapter 13 plan feasibility, budget schedules, trustee review, and attorney strategy. Support staff can treat financial information as evidence, not casual estimates. A debtor's memory may be incomplete. Pay stubs, tax returns, bank statements, benefit letters, business records, payment apps, and profit-and-loss reports can tell different parts of the story.

Schedule I reports current income. Schedule J reports current expenses. These schedules are not the same as a bank statement summary or a tax return. They are bankruptcy disclosures that needs to be completed using attorney-approved methods and client information. Staff can identify gross income, deductions, payroll frequency, overtime, bonuses, commissions, self-employment income, benefits, contributions, support, household contributions, and irregular income.

The means test is a statutory calculation used in many consumer cases. It generally looks at income over a defined period and allowed expense categories. The result may affect Chapter 7 eligibility or create review issues. A support professional may enter pay data and organize documents, but may not decide whether the debtor passes, fails, or can change chapters. If pay stubs are missing or income is irregular, the file is best handled as flagged.

Self-employment income requires special care. A debtor may deposit gross receipts into a personal account, pay expenses in cash, use payment apps, or mix business and personal funds. Tax returns may be outdated. Bank statements may show deposits that need explanation. A simple monthly average may be misleading if the business is seasonal. Support staff can gather source records and prepare a clean summary for attorney review.

Expense review also matters. Housing, utilities, food, transportation, insurance, medical expenses, childcare, taxes, support, payroll deductions, business expenses, and debt payments needs to be handled consistently. Some expenses shown in bank statements may not belong on a schedule in the same way. Some debts paid directly may be treated differently in a Chapter 13 plan. Attorney instructions control.

The strongest quality check is reconciliation. Compare questionnaire income to pay stubs. Compare deposits to reported income. Compare tax returns to business records. Compare vehicle expenses to listed vehicles. Compare mortgage or rent expenses to leases and statements. Any unexplained difference is best handled as written as a question, not silently corrected.

Key Terms

Click a term card and explain it out loud before checking yourself.

Primary Source Focus

These are the official-law reading anchors for the chapter.

  • 11 U.S.C. secs. 101(10A), 707, 1325.
  • Official Forms B106I, B106J, B122A, B122C.
  • Current official means test instructions and standards.

Practice Scenario

Irregular Income

Facts: The debtor reports $4,000 monthly income. Pay stubs show base pay of $3,200, overtime in two months, and a bonus. Bank statements also show payment app deposits from weekend repair work.

Support staff avoid choosing the final income number. The file can summarize all income sources, identify periods covered, flag side income, request business details if needed, and ask attorney how to treat overtime, bonus, and payment app deposits.

Workflow: What You Do in Practice

1

Prepare an income source chart with employer, pay frequency, gross income, deductions, period covered, and missing pay stubs.

2

Identify non-wage deposits in bank statements.

3

Compare tax return income with current income and explain differences when known.

4

Prepare expense source notes and flag unsupported or unusual expenses.

Red Flags to Escalate

  • Missing pay stubs in the means test lookback period.
  • Large deposits without explanation.
  • Business income reported with no expense records.
  • Budget shows negative disposable income but Chapter 13 plan is expected.

Practice Assignment

Use these as workbook tasks. A trainer or attorney reviewer can grade them using the capstone rubric.

  • Use sample pay stubs to create a six-month gross income worksheet.
  • Review a bank statement and list unexplained deposits.
  • Draft five attorney-review questions about inconsistent income and expenses.

Knowledge Check

Choose an answer. The book gives immediate feedback and tracks your score inside this browser.

1. Which schedule reports current income?

2. Which schedule reports current expenses?

3. Who decides the legal effect of the means test?

4. What can staff do with unexplained deposits?

5. Self-employment income may require extra records.

My Notes

Chapter 8

Chapter 7 Practice from Intake to Discharge

8 hours

Learning Map

Before reading, use this map to see what the chapter helps you do in practice.

  • Follow a Chapter 7 consumer workflow from intake to closing.
  • Understand no-asset and asset case concepts.
  • Prepare trustee document support and 341 meeting support.
  • Track discharge and closing requirements.

Read the Lesson

Chapter 7 is often the first chapter new bankruptcy support staff learn because the workflow is relatively clear. A case begins with intake and document collection. The team gathers identity information, income records, tax returns, bank statements, creditor data, asset information, lawsuits, transfers, and required certificates. The attorney reviews eligibility, chapter choice, exemptions, risk issues, and client strategy. Draft forms are prepared, reviewed, signed, and filed.

After filing, a Chapter 7 trustee is assigned and a meeting of creditors is scheduled. The trustee reviews the petition, schedules, Statement of Financial Affairs, tax returns, pay information, bank statements, asset records, transfers, and any other documents required by local practice. Many consumer Chapter 7 cases are no-asset cases, meaning the trustee does not expect a distribution to unsecured creditors. Some cases are asset cases, where nonexempt property or recoveries may be administered.

The 341 meeting is not a casual appointment. The debtor answers questions under oath. Support staff may help prepare document packets, confirm meeting information, track identification requirements according to attorney instructions, and log trustee requests. Staff avoid coach testimony. They may communicate administrative reminders approved by the attorney.

Common Chapter 7 issues include missing bank statements, inconsistent income, undisclosed assets, recent transfers, tax refunds, vehicle equity, mortgage intentions, reaffirmation agreements, domestic support obligations, lawsuits, and creditor disputes. The support team can maintain an issue list from intake through discharge.

Reaffirmation and statement of intention work is high risk. A debtor may want to keep a vehicle or other collateral. The legal consequences of reaffirming debt needs to be explained by an attorney. Staff can track whether documents were received, deadlines, signatures, and hearing notices, but cannot recommend reaffirmation.

Discharge and closing are not the same. A discharge affects personal liability for dischargeable debts. Closing is the administrative end of the case. A case may be closed without discharge or may remain open for asset administration after discharge. Support staff can read the docket carefully and avoid telling a client that every matter is finished unless the attorney has approved the communication.

Key Terms

Click a term card and explain it out loud before checking yourself.

Primary Source Focus

These are the official-law reading anchors for the chapter.

  • U.S. Courts Chapter 7 Basics.
  • 11 U.S.C. secs. 521, 341, 704, 707, 727.
  • Official Forms B101, B106 series, B107, B108, B122A.

Practice Scenario

Trustee Request After 341 Meeting

Facts: After the meeting, the trustee requests bank statements for the filing date, proof of vehicle value, and documents about a tax refund.

Support staff can log the request, due date, requested documents, assigned owner, and attorney instructions. They can request documents from the client using approved language and flag any inability to provide documents before the deadline.

Workflow: What You Do in Practice

1

Maintain a Chapter 7 intake-to-discharge checklist.

2

Prepare a trustee document packet according to local and attorney requirements.

3

Track meeting date, trustee requests, debtor education certificate, reaffirmation documents, objections, discharge, and closing.

4

Update the client communication log with approved administrative messages.

Red Flags to Escalate

  • Tax refund not disclosed or not valued.
  • Client transferred a vehicle or repaid family before filing.
  • Missing debtor education certificate close to discharge timing.
  • Creditor contacts debtor after filing or discharge.

Practice Assignment

Use these as workbook tasks. A trainer or attorney reviewer can grade them using the capstone rubric.

  • Create a Chapter 7 timeline with 15 events from intake to closing.
  • Prepare a trustee document request tracker.
  • Write safe client instructions for uploading bank statements, without giving testimony advice.

Knowledge Check

Choose an answer. The book gives immediate feedback and tracks your score inside this browser.

1. Who is assigned to administer a Chapter 7 case?

2. What is a 341 meeting?

3. A no-asset case means what generally?

4. Who explains reaffirmation consequences?

5. Discharge and closing are always identical.

My Notes

Chapter 9

Chapter 13 Practice from Plan Drafting to Case Completion

8 hours

Learning Map

Before reading, use this map to see what the chapter helps you do in practice.

  • Explain the practical purpose of a Chapter 13 plan.
  • Identify plan data needed for secured, priority, and unsecured claims.
  • Track confirmation, claims, trustee objections, and plan modifications.
  • Support postconfirmation administration.

Read the Lesson

Chapter 13 is a long-term case for eligible individuals with regular income. Instead of focusing mainly on liquidation, Chapter 13 focuses on a plan. The debtor proposes payments over a period of time, and the plan explains how different creditors will be treated. A Chapter 13 case may help address mortgage arrears, vehicle debt, priority taxes, attorney fees, domestic support obligations, and unsecured debt. The legal design of the plan belongs to the attorney, but support staff provide the data that makes the plan possible.

Plan preparation depends on accurate income, expenses, assets, secured debt balances, arrears, collateral values, tax debt, domestic support information, and creditor addresses. The team can collect mortgage statements, payment histories, escrow notices, vehicle loan statements, proof of insurance, tax notices, pay stubs, bank statements, and budget information. If the debtor is behind on mortgage payments, the arrears number needs to be supported and updated.

Confirmation is court approval of the plan. Before confirmation, the trustee or creditors may object. Objections may concern feasibility, disposable income, plan terms, secured debt treatment, missing tax returns, insurance, valuation, or inaccurate claims. Support staff can summarize objections in neutral issue lists and gather documents needed for attorney response.

Claims are critical in Chapter 13. Creditors file proofs of claim. The trustee may pay according to allowed claims and the confirmed plan. Staff can compare claims to schedules and plan treatment, identify missing claims, identify duplicates, and flag unexpected amounts. Home mortgage claims and notices under Rule 3002.1 require careful tracking because payment changes and fees can affect the debtor's ongoing obligations.

After confirmation, the case continues for years. That is where many BPO teams add value. Tasks include monitoring notices of mortgage payment change, notices of postpetition fees, trustee motions to dismiss, debtor motions to modify, claim transfers, tax refund issues, payment defaults, employer wage order changes, address changes, and discharge readiness. A Chapter 13 case is not done just because the plan is confirmed.

Communication discipline is especially important in Chapter 13. A client may ask whether to skip a trustee payment, buy a car, sell property, refinance, incur new debt, or change jobs. These are legal and strategic questions. Staff can collect the facts and route the question to the attorney.

Key Terms

Click a term card and explain it out loud before checking yourself.

Primary Source Focus

These are the official-law reading anchors for the chapter.

  • U.S. Courts Chapter 13 Basics.
  • 11 U.S.C. secs. 1322, 1325, 1327, 1328, 1329.
  • Bankruptcy Rules 3002.1 and 3015.

Practice Scenario

Trustee Objection

Facts: The trustee objects that the plan is not feasible and tax returns are missing. The debtor says income has increased since filing.

Support staff can gather updated pay stubs, tax return status, budget changes, and plan payment history. The issue list can state the objection grounds and documents collected, then route to attorney for response and possible amended plan analysis.

Workflow: What You Do in Practice

1

Create a plan data worksheet with income, expenses, secured debts, arrears, priority claims, attorney fees, and unsecured debt pool.

2

Track confirmation hearing, objection deadlines, amended plans, and trustee recommendations.

3

Maintain a claims comparison sheet after the claims bar date.

4

Monitor postconfirmation notices and motions until case completion.

Red Flags to Escalate

  • Mortgage arrears not supported by current statement or claim.
  • Plan payment higher than budget appears to support.
  • Missing tax returns or domestic support certification issues.
  • Client wants to incur new debt during the case.

Practice Assignment

Use these as workbook tasks. A trainer or attorney reviewer can grade them using the capstone rubric.

  • Build a Chapter 13 plan data packet from sample mortgage, vehicle, tax, and income records.
  • Summarize a trustee objection in five bullet points for attorney review.
  • Design a postconfirmation monitoring checklist.

Knowledge Check

Choose an answer. The book gives immediate feedback and tracks your score inside this browser.

1. What is confirmation?

2. Why are claims important in Chapter 13?

3. Who decides how a plan treats creditors?

4. Chapter 13 cases often require monitoring after confirmation.

5. A client asking to buy a car during Chapter 13 is best handled as routed to whom?

My Notes

Chapter 10

Chapter 11, Subchapter V, and Business Bankruptcy

8 hours

Learning Map

Before reading, use this map to see what the chapter helps you do in practice.

  • Explain Chapter 11 reorganization in practical terms.
  • Identify first-day motions and business debtor documents.
  • Understand debtor-in-possession concepts.
  • Support Subchapter V case organization.

Read the Lesson

Chapter 11 is the major reorganization chapter. It is used by businesses and sometimes individuals with complex debt. The basic idea is that the debtor seeks breathing room to reorganize, sell assets, restructure obligations, or liquidate in an orderly way while under court supervision. Unlike Chapter 7, where a trustee commonly administers the estate, a Chapter 11 debtor often remains in possession of its business and assets as a debtor in possession.

A debtor in possession has serious duties. It may continue operating the business, but it needs to comply with bankruptcy law, court orders, U.S. Trustee requirements, reporting duties, and fiduciary responsibilities. Business cases involve payroll, bank accounts, cash management, vendors, leases, insurance, taxes, secured lenders, contracts, customer obligations, and financial reporting. This makes Chapter 11 document-intensive from the first day.

First-day motions are early requests designed to stabilize operations. A debtor may seek authority to pay wages, maintain bank accounts, honor customer programs, use cash collateral, pay critical vendors, maintain insurance, pay taxes, reject or assume certain obligations later, or obtain financing. Support staff may prepare exhibit binders, vendor lists, employee summaries, bank account charts, insurance schedules, lease indexes, and service lists.

Cash collateral is a major Chapter 11 concept. If cash or receivables are subject to a secured creditor's interest, the debtor may need consent or court authority to use them. This is legal and financial work. Support staff can organize loan documents, UCC filings, bank statements, collateral descriptions, budgets, and creditor communications, but cannot negotiate use or promise outcomes.

A Chapter 11 plan classifies claims and proposes treatment. A disclosure statement may provide information to creditors for voting, unless an exception applies. Subchapter V provides a streamlined path for qualifying small business debtors and has different timing and trustee features. Support teams needs to track deadlines carefully because business cases move fast and involve many parties.

Business bankruptcy support requires a higher level of indexing. A single business debtor file may include thousands of documents. The team can use standardized folder structures: corporate authority, financials, bank accounts, secured debt, leases, contracts, litigation, taxes, employees, vendors, insurance, real estate, equipment, monthly operating reports, and court filings.

Key Terms

Click a term card and explain it out loud before checking yourself.

Primary Source Focus

These are the official-law reading anchors for the chapter.

  • U.S. Courts Chapter 11 Basics.
  • 11 U.S.C. secs. 1101, 1107, 1108, 1121, 1122, 1123, 1125, 1129, 1181-1195.
  • U.S. Trustee Chapter 11 operating guidance.

Practice Scenario

Restaurant Chapter 11 Intake

Facts: A restaurant LLC has 42 employees, two locations, unpaid rent, a secured equipment lender, tax debt, gift card obligations, and a frozen bank account.

Support staff can create a business intake index: corporate records, leases, payroll, employee list, bank accounts, tax notices, secured loan documents, equipment list, insurance, vendor list, gift card liabilities, lawsuits, and cash flow. Frozen account and payroll facts require immediate attorney review.

Workflow: What You Do in Practice

1

Build first-day motion support trackers.

2

Index contracts, leases, secured debt, employees, vendors, taxes, litigation, and insurance.

3

Track U.S. Trustee reporting requirements and monthly operating reports.

4

Maintain service lists for major parties.

Red Flags to Escalate

  • Payroll due before filing or immediately after filing.
  • Cash collateral use without clear authority.
  • Insurance lapse, tax deadlines, locked bank accounts, or utility shutoff.
  • Unclear authority for an entity to file.

Practice Assignment

Use these as workbook tasks. A trainer or attorney reviewer can grade them using the capstone rubric.

  • Create a Chapter 11 first-day document request list.
  • Prepare a contract and lease tracker for a fictional small business.
  • Summarize what information belongs in a monthly operating report package.

Knowledge Check

Choose an answer. The book gives immediate feedback and tracks your score inside this browser.

1. What is a debtor in possession?

2. What is a first-day motion?

3. Cash collateral issues is best handled as escalated to whom?

4. Subchapter V is connected to what?

5. Business bankruptcy files require strong indexing.

My Notes

Chapter 11

Discharge, Nondischargeability, and Reaffirmation

6 hours

Learning Map

Before reading, use this map to see what the chapter helps you do in practice.

  • Explain discharge carefully and accurately.
  • Recognize debts that require dischargeability review.
  • Understand reaffirmation as a high-risk postfiling issue.
  • Track discharge readiness without giving legal advice.

Read the Lesson

Discharge is one of the main goals in many consumer bankruptcy cases, but it is often misunderstood. A discharge is a court order that affects the debtor's personal liability for discharged debts. It does not necessarily remove liens, erase every kind of debt, undo every collection record, or mean the case is fully closed. A support professional can use careful language and avoid saying: all debts are gone.

Some debts are not discharged automatically or may require special litigation. Domestic support obligations, certain taxes, student loans unless the required standard is met, debts arising from fraud, willful and malicious injury, certain fines or restitution, and other categories may need legal review. A debt description in a questionnaire may trigger review even before the attorney has decided the issue.

Discharge can also be challenged. A creditor or trustee may object to discharge or dischargeability based on alleged misconduct, false statements, concealment, fraud, or other grounds. These matters may become adversary proceedings. Staff can identify complaint deadlines, docket pleadings, preserve documents, and route litigation issues quickly.

Reaffirmation agreements are common in Chapter 7 vehicle cases and sometimes other secured debt cases. By reaffirming, a debtor may remain personally liable on a debt that might otherwise be discharged. This can help keep collateral in some circumstances but can also create serious future risk. Because reaffirmation affects legal rights after bankruptcy, support staff cannot recommend it. They may track forms, signatures, deadlines, creditor communications, and hearing notices.

Discharge readiness includes administrative and legal components. Staff may track debtor education certificates, filing of required documents, objections deadlines, trustee requests, reaffirmation status, and docket entries. But the attorney decides whether there are unresolved legal problems before communicating final advice to the client.

Post-discharge creditor contact is best handled as handled carefully. Some creditor communications are informational, some concern surviving liens, and some may be improper collection attempts. Support staff can collect the communication, date, sender, account, debt type, bankruptcy case information, and any payment demand language, then escalate for review.

Key Terms

Click a term card and explain it out loud before checking yourself.

Primary Source Focus

These are the official-law reading anchors for the chapter.

  • 11 U.S.C. secs. 523, 524, 727, 1328.
  • Bankruptcy Rules 4004 and 4007.
  • Official reaffirmation forms.

Practice Scenario

Student Loan Question

Facts: A debtor asks whether bankruptcy will remove $80,000 in student loans. The intake questionnaire also lists credit card debts and medical bills.

Support staff avoid answering dischargeability. The note can say: Client asks student loan discharge question; debt listed as student loan approximately $80,000; attorney review required. Collect loan statements and any hardship-related facts requested by attorney.

Workflow: What You Do in Practice

1

Track debtor education completion and certificate filing.

2

Flag debts described as support, tax, student loan, fraud, fines, restitution, injury, or recent luxury purchases.

3

Maintain reaffirmation deadline and signature tracker.

4

Log post-discharge creditor communications for attorney review.

Red Flags to Escalate

  • Client believes every debt will disappear.
  • Creditor complaint or adversary proceeding filed.
  • Reaffirmation form near deadline or incomplete.
  • Collection demand after discharge.

Practice Assignment

Use these as workbook tasks. A trainer or attorney reviewer can grade them using the capstone rubric.

  • Prepare a discharge readiness checklist.
  • Rewrite five unsafe discharge statements into safe administrative statements.
  • Summarize the difference between discharge and lien release.

Knowledge Check

Choose an answer. The book gives immediate feedback and tracks your score inside this browser.

1. Does discharge automatically remove every lien?

2. Who can explain reaffirmation consequences?

3. Which debt often requires special discharge review?

4. What can staff do with post-discharge collection letters?

5. Debtor education may be required before discharge.

My Notes

Chapter 12

Bankruptcy Litigation, Motions, and Adversary Proceedings

7 hours

Learning Map

Before reading, use this map to see what the chapter helps you do in practice.

  • Distinguish motions, contested matters, and adversary proceedings.
  • Understand common bankruptcy litigation types.
  • Support avoidance action document review.
  • Track litigation deadlines and service.

Read the Lesson

Bankruptcy is not only forms and schedules. It is also litigation. Some disputes are handled by motion practice as contested matters. Others require an adversary proceeding, which is similar to a separate lawsuit connected to the bankruptcy case. Rule 7001 identifies categories that generally require an adversary proceeding, such as certain recovery actions, lien disputes, dischargeability complaints, and injunction matters. Support staff needs to read the filing type carefully because deadlines and procedures differ.

Common motions include relief from stay, dismissal, conversion, use of cash collateral, sale of property, abandonment, assumption or rejection of leases, claim objections, plan confirmation, plan modification, and fee applications. A motion usually asks the court to do something. A notice may set an objection deadline or hearing date. An order tells parties what the court decided. The support task is to convert the document into calendar entries, document requests, and attorney-review notes.

Avoidance actions are a major bankruptcy litigation area. A trustee or debtor in possession may seek to avoid certain transfers made before or after filing. Preferences often involve payments to creditors before bankruptcy. Fraudulent transfer claims may involve transfers for less than reasonably equivalent value or transfers made with problematic intent. Support staff do not decide whether a claim exists. They build transaction timelines from bank statements, checks, wire records, titles, contracts, invoices, and relationship information.

Litigation support requires evidence discipline. Documents needs to be named consistently, stored in the correct matter, and preserved. Emails, bank records, contracts, invoices, payment histories, and pleadings may become exhibits. Privileged communications needs to be handled under policy. If a learner is unsure whether a document is privileged or confidential, the correct answer is to escalate.

Deadlines in litigation can be unforgiving. A complaint has answer deadlines. A motion has response deadlines. Discovery has response due dates. Hearings require preparation. Service needs to be correct. Many local rules use negative notice procedures, where relief may be granted if no objection is filed by a deadline. Support staff can maintain a litigation calendar separate from general case milestones.

Good litigation summaries are neutral. They do not argue like a lawyer unless the attorney asks for a draft. They identify who filed what, what relief is requested, what facts are alleged, what deadlines exist, what documents are attached, what documents are missing, and what questions need attorney review.

Key Terms

Click a term card and explain it out loud before checking yourself.

Primary Source Focus

These are the official-law reading anchors for the chapter.

  • 11 U.S.C. secs. 544, 547, 548, 549, 550, 553.
  • Bankruptcy Rules 7001-7087 and 9014.
  • Local motion practice and negative notice rules.

Practice Scenario

Preference Timeline

Facts: A debtor paid $12,000 to a credit card company 45 days before filing and $8,000 to her father 10 months before filing.

Support staff can create a transfer timeline with dates, amounts, payees, relationship, source accounts, debt description, and documents. The father payment is best handled as flagged as insider-related for attorney review. Staff avoid label either payment legally avoidable.

Workflow: What You Do in Practice

1

Create motion summaries with requested relief, filer, response deadline, hearing date, and documents attached.

2

Maintain adversary proceeding trackers with complaint, answer date, discovery, motions, hearings, and status.

3

Build transfer timelines from bank statements and transaction records.

4

Apply naming conventions and confidentiality labels.

Red Flags to Escalate

  • Complaint or summons received.
  • Negative notice deadline approaching.
  • Transfer to insider before filing.
  • Document request includes privileged or sensitive information.

Practice Assignment

Use these as workbook tasks. A trainer or attorney reviewer can grade them using the capstone rubric.

  • Summarize a sample motion for relief from stay.
  • Build an adversary proceeding calendar from a sample complaint and summons.
  • Prepare a transfer timeline from a fictional bank statement.

Knowledge Check

Choose an answer. The book gives immediate feedback and tracks your score inside this browser.

1. What is an adversary proceeding?

2. What usually starts a contested matter?

3. Who decides if a transfer is avoidable?

4. Why are negative notice deadlines important?

5. Litigation summaries is best handled as neutral and deadline-focused.

My Notes

Chapter 13

Official Forms, Petition Drafting, and Quality Control

9 hours

Learning Map

Before reading, use this map to see what the chapter helps you do in practice.

  • Identify the major consumer bankruptcy forms.
  • Map source documents to form fields.
  • Run consistency and completeness checks.
  • Prepare attorney-review notes for unresolved issues.

Read the Lesson

Official Bankruptcy Forms are the backbone of petition production. Software can make forms look easy, but it can also hide errors. A learner needs to understand the purpose of each form. The voluntary petition identifies the debtor, chapter, filing information, prior cases, and basic case data. The schedules disclose assets, debts, codebtors, income, expenses, contracts, and leases. The Statement of Financial Affairs discloses financial history. Means test forms or Chapter 13 calculation forms provide income and expense calculations. The creditor matrix supports notice.

Form preparation begins before typing. First, collect source documents and client responses. Second, index them. Third, map each data point to the correct form. Fourth, enter data consistently. Fifth, run quality control. Sixth, prepare attorney-review questions. Seventh, revise after attorney instructions. Eighth, prepare signature and filing materials according to office policy.

Schedule A/B can connect to deeds, titles, statements, appraisals, bank records, insurance schedules, business records, and client responses. Schedule C depends on attorney-selected exemptions. Schedule D lists secured claims. Schedule E/F lists priority and nonpriority unsecured claims. Schedule G lists executory contracts and leases. Schedule H lists codebtors. Schedule I and J disclose income and expenses. SOFA asks about prior income, payments, lawsuits, transfers, repossessions, gifts, losses, business interests, and more.

The most common production errors are not exotic legal mistakes. They are mismatched names, missing aliases, wrong addresses, omitted creditors, inconsistent asset values, missing liens, duplicated debts, missing lawsuits, unlisted transfers, wrong date ranges, incorrect county, unredacted identifiers, and signatures on stale drafts. These errors can be reduced with a source-to-form checklist.

Quality control is best handled as layered. The preparer checks completion. A reviewer checks consistency and source support. The attorney reviews legal judgment. In BPO environments, a separate QC analyst may review before attorney delivery. Critical errors is best handled as tracked and used for training. A good culture treats corrections as process improvement, not personal embarrassment.

Attorney-review notes is best handled as concise. A poor note says: please review everything. A useful note says: Schedule A/B lists 2019 Honda value at $11,000 from client estimate; loan statement shows balance $14,200; no valuation source received; attorney to confirm value source and exemption treatment. Good notes save attorney time and improve client protection.

Key Terms

Click a term card and explain it out loud before checking yourself.

Primary Source Focus

These are the official-law reading anchors for the chapter.

  • Official Bankruptcy Forms page.
  • Official Forms B101, B106A/B-B106J, B107, B108, B122A, B122C.
  • Bankruptcy Rules 1007, 1008, 9011, 9037.

Practice Scenario

Form Consistency Error

Facts: The petition lists the debtor's address in County A. The mortgage statement and deed show the home in County B. The client recently moved but still owns the home.

Support staff avoid silently choosing one county. The review note can identify petition address, property address, deed county, move date if known, and ask attorney to confirm venue, residence, mailing address, and real property schedule treatment.

Workflow: What You Do in Practice

1

Use a source-to-form checklist for each schedule.

2

Run name, address, county, creditor, asset, lien, income, expense, transfer, lawsuit, and redaction checks.

3

Prepare a form completion report before attorney review.

4

Maintain version control for drafts sent for signature.

Red Flags to Escalate

  • Client signs a draft before final attorney review.
  • Form contains full Social Security number where redaction is required.
  • Asset listed without matching lien or value source.
  • SOFA responses conflict with bank statements or lawsuits.

Practice Assignment

Use these as workbook tasks. A trainer or attorney reviewer can grade them using the capstone rubric.

  • Create a source-to-form map for Schedules A/B, D, E/F, I, J, and SOFA.
  • Perform a 50-point QC review on a sample petition.
  • Write five attorney-review notes from sample inconsistencies.

Knowledge Check

Choose an answer. The book gives immediate feedback and tracks your score inside this browser.

1. What does SOFA stand for?

2. What is the purpose of source-to-form mapping?

3. Which rule is important for privacy redaction?

4. Who reviews legal judgment before filing?

5. Quality control can check consistency across forms.

My Notes

Chapter 14

Docketing, PACER, CM/ECF, Notices, and Deadlines

7 hours

Learning Map

Before reading, use this map to see what the chapter helps you do in practice.

  • Read a bankruptcy docket for practical action items.
  • Extract deadlines from notices, motions, orders, and claims.
  • Understand PACER and CM/ECF access discipline.
  • Build a docket review SOP.

Read the Lesson

The docket is the official activity record of a bankruptcy case. It shows petitions, schedules, notices, motions, orders, claims events, hearings, certificates, objections, amendments, dismissals, discharges, and closings. Docket review is one of the most valuable support skills because every filing can create a deadline, task, or risk. A support worker who reads dockets well can prevent missed hearings, missed objections, and missed document requests.

PACER provides access to federal court records. CM/ECF is the electronic filing system used by federal courts. Law firms control who may access accounts, download documents, file documents, and incur fees. BPO teams needs to follow strict credential, billing, confidentiality, and filing authorization procedures. No one can file documents using credentials unless specifically authorized and trained under the firm's rules.

A docket entry is best handled as converted into structured data. The support team can capture docket number, filed date, filer, document title, related document, hearing date, response deadline, service requirement, assigned attorney, and next action. If a deadline is uncertain, do not guess quietly. Mark it for review. Deadline uncertainty needs to be visible.

Notices can be deceptively simple. A notice of meeting of creditors provides meeting information and deadlines. A notice of deficiency may require a corrected filing. A notice of returned mail may indicate a creditor did not receive notice. A notice of mortgage payment change may affect Chapter 13 plan administration. A notice of transfer of claim may change creditor contact details. Each notice is best handled as read for action, not merely stored.

Orders are controlling documents. If an order grants relief from stay, approves a sale, confirms a plan, dismisses a case, converts a case, or requires documents, the support team needs to update the case status and task list. Orders avoid be summarized casually. The exact relief and deadlines matter.

Good docketing systems use redundancy for high-risk dates. A team may use a primary calendar, case management system, reviewer initials, and daily deadline reports. BPO operations can define who reviews new docket entries, how often, who calculates deadlines, who verifies them, and how escalations are documented.

Key Terms

Click a term card and explain it out loud before checking yourself.

Primary Source Focus

These are the official-law reading anchors for the chapter.

  • Bankruptcy Rules 2002, 5005, 9006, 9011, 9036, 9037.
  • Local CM/ECF procedures.
  • Firm credential and filing policies.

Practice Scenario

Returned Mail

Facts: A docket entry says notice to ABC Collections was returned as undeliverable. ABC is listed as a large unsecured creditor.

Support staff can log returned mail, identify the creditor, compare matrix address to source documents, search approved sources under policy, and escalate for attorney instruction on amended notice or address correction.

Workflow: What You Do in Practice

1

Review new docket entries daily or according to client SLA.

2

Extract deadlines and hearings into the case calendar.

3

Download and name documents using a standard naming convention.

4

Escalate orders, motions, objections, deficiencies, returned mail, and trustee requests.

Red Flags to Escalate

  • Notice of deficiency.
  • Order dismissing, converting, or granting stay relief.
