  • Motion with response deadline under negative notice.
  • Returned mail for major creditor or trustee.

Practice Assignment

Use these as workbook tasks. A trainer or attorney reviewer can grade them using the capstone rubric.

  • Create a docket review SOP for a BPO team.
  • Turn ten sample docket entries into a deadline/action spreadsheet.
  • Draft a naming convention for downloaded bankruptcy PDFs.

Knowledge Check

Choose an answer. The book gives immediate feedback and tracks your score inside this browser.

1. What is the docket?

2. What can staff extract from a motion?

3. Can credentials be shared casually?

4. What does a returned mail notice require?

5. Orders is best handled as read carefully because they control obligations.

My Notes

Chapter 15

Ethics, Unauthorized Practice of Law, Confidentiality, and Data Security

7 hours

Learning Map

Before reading, use this map to see what the chapter helps you do in practice.

  • Recognize unauthorized practice of law risks.
  • Use safe communication boundaries with clients.
  • Protect confidential and sensitive information.
  • Escalate conflicts, privilege, and data incidents.

Read the Lesson

Bankruptcy support work sits close to legal advice. That makes ethics training essential. A support professional may know a lot about bankruptcy, but knowledge does not create authority to advise clients. Unauthorized practice of law can occur when a non-lawyer recommends a legal strategy, interprets legal rights for a client, selects exemptions, chooses a chapter, predicts court outcomes, tells a client whether a debt is dischargeable, or negotiates legal rights without authorization.

Safe communication is clear and limited. Staff may say: We received your pay stubs. The attorney needs your most recent mortgage statement. Your meeting is scheduled for this date and time. Please upload the tax return requested. Staff avoid say: You can file Chapter 13. This debt will be wiped out. You do not need to list that asset. The creditor is violating the stay. You can stop paying that loan. Those statements involve legal judgment.

Confidentiality is a professional obligation and an operational discipline. Bankruptcy files contain Social Security numbers, bank account numbers, tax returns, medical bills, payroll data, business records, domestic support information, lawsuits, and family details. Access is best handled as limited to people who need the information to perform assigned work. Documents avoid be downloaded, copied, emailed, printed, or stored outside approved systems.

Privacy rules and redaction procedures are especially important. Filings needs to protect certain personal identifiers under applicable rules. Staff can know the firm's redaction workflow and can never rely on visual appearance alone. A black box placed over text may not remove underlying data unless the PDF is properly redacted. Redaction errors are serious.

Data incidents needs to be escalated quickly. A misdirected email, wrong upload, lost device, unauthorized download, or exposed full identifier avoid be hidden. The correct response is to follow incident policy immediately: preserve facts, notify the supervisor or security contact, stop further disclosure if possible, and document what happened. Delay can make harm worse.

Ethics also includes honesty in production. Do not change dates, hide missing documents, backfill checklists, use another person's credentials, mark attorney review complete when it is not complete, or pressure a client to sign something they have not reviewed. High-quality BPO work depends on trust.

Key Terms

Click a term card and explain it out loud before checking yourself.

Primary Source Focus

These are the official-law reading anchors for the chapter.

  • Bankruptcy Rules 9011 and 9037.
  • Applicable attorney ethics rules and paralegal guidelines.
  • Firm information security and confidentiality policies.

Practice Scenario

Unsafe Client Question

Facts: A client asks: can I transfer my car to my sister before filing so the trustee cannot take it?

Support staff avoid answering the strategy question. A safe response is: I cannot advise you on transfers or legal strategy. I will send this question to the attorney for review. The internal note can quote the question, identify the car, timing, sister relationship, and any documents.

Workflow: What You Do in Practice

1

Use approved client scripts.

2

Route legal questions to attorneys.

3

Apply redaction and secure transfer procedures.

4

Report data incidents immediately.

Red Flags to Escalate

  • Client asks whether to transfer property, omit a creditor, stop paying, or choose a chapter.
  • Full Social Security number appears in a draft filing.
  • Staff member asks to use another person's filing credentials.
  • Documents sent to the wrong recipient.

Practice Assignment

Use these as workbook tasks. A trainer or attorney reviewer can grade them using the capstone rubric.

  • Rewrite ten risky client statements into safe administrative responses.
  • Create a redaction checklist for bankruptcy filings.
  • Prepare a one-page incident report for a fictional misdirected tax return.

Knowledge Check

Choose an answer. The book gives immediate feedback and tracks your score inside this browser.

1. May staff choose exemptions for a debtor?

2. What is a safe response to a legal advice question?

3. What can happen after a misdirected email with sensitive documents?

4. Why is redaction important?

5. Using someone else's filing credentials is acceptable.

My Notes

Chapter 16

Capstone: Building an Attorney-Review-Ready Bankruptcy File

12 to 15 hours

Learning Map

Before reading, use this map to see what the chapter helps you do in practice.

  • Apply the full course to a simulated case file.
  • Prepare a document index, issue log, creditor worksheet, asset log, and attorney memo.
  • Identify legal questions without answering them.
  • Demonstrate readiness for supervised bankruptcy support work.

Read the Lesson

The capstone is the point where the course becomes job training. A learner receives a simulated debtor file and needs to turn it into a reviewable package. The goal is not to file a real case. The goal is to demonstrate that the learner can organize facts, connect documents to forms, identify missing items, catch inconsistencies, and communicate issues clearly to an attorney.

The simulated debtor is an individual wage earner with a spouse, two children, a home with mortgage arrears, one vehicle loan, medical bills, credit card debt, an old tax debt, student loans, a small side business, a recent wage garnishment, a pending state court collection case, and a transfer of a motorcycle to a relative six months before filing. The file includes pay stubs, partial bank statements, mortgage statements, a vehicle loan statement, collection letters, a credit report excerpt, tax notice, lawsuit papers, and a questionnaire with inconsistent answers.

The first deliverable is a document index. Each document is best handled as named, dated, categorized, and connected to the issue it supports. A pay stub supports income. A mortgage statement supports secured debt and arrears. A lawsuit complaint supports SOFA litigation disclosure and creditor classification. A tax notice supports priority or discharge review. A bank statement may support income, transfers, expenses, and asset balances.

The second deliverable is an issue log. The issue log avoid contain legal conclusions. It can list facts requiring review: foreclosure risk, garnishment, tax debt, student loans, recent transfer to relative, missing bank statements, side business income, mortgage arrears, vehicle valuation, lawsuit, and possible domestic support if facts show it. For each issue, the learner can identify source documents, missing documents, and attorney questions.

The third deliverable is a draft schedule map. This is not a final petition. It is a worksheet showing where facts may appear: assets on Schedule A/B, possible exemptions on Schedule C for attorney selection, secured debts on Schedule D, priority and unsecured debts on Schedule E/F, contracts on Schedule G, codebtors on Schedule H, income on Schedule I, expenses on Schedule J, and financial history on SOFA.

The final deliverable is an attorney-review memo. The memo is best handled as short, organized, and useful. It can begin with case posture and urgent deadlines, then list major issues, missing documents, and recommended review questions. It can end with a quality-control status: what has been verified, what remains missing, and what cannot be completed without attorney instruction or client documents.

Key Terms

Click a term card and explain it out loud before checking yourself.

Primary Source Focus

These are the official-law reading anchors for the chapter.

  • Review all prior official sources.
  • Official Forms B101, B106 series, B107, B122A or B122C as assigned.
  • Local rules selected by trainer.

Practice Scenario

Attorney-Review Note

Facts: Bank statement shows $2,800 paid to debtor's brother six months before filing. Questionnaire says no transfers. Debtor later says it was repayment for a loan.

A good memo entry: Possible transfer/insider repayment issue. Source: Bank of Example statement dated [date], ACH/check to brother for $2,800. Conflict: questionnaire says no transfers. Client later described repayment of family loan. Missing: loan documents, payment history, brother relationship confirmation, attorney instruction on SOFA/avoidance review.

Workflow: What You Do in Practice

1

Create document index, missing document list, creditor worksheet, asset log, income worksheet, issue log, and attorney memo.

2

Mark every fact with a source or client-response label.

3

Use escalation language for legal issues.

4

Complete a final QC review before submission.

Red Flags to Escalate

  • Urgent garnishment or foreclosure deadline.
  • Recent transfer to relative.
  • Conflicting questionnaire and bank statement facts.
  • Student loan, tax debt, and side business income requiring attorney review.

Practice Assignment

Use these as workbook tasks. A trainer or attorney reviewer can grade them using the capstone rubric.

  • Complete the full capstone package.
  • Take the final exam using the answer key only after completion.
  • Write a reflection identifying three SOP improvements that would prevent errors in this file.

Knowledge Check

Choose an answer. The book gives immediate feedback and tracks your score inside this browser.

1. What is the capstone's goal?

2. How can recent transfer facts be handled?

3. What can every factual entry connect to?

4. What belongs in an attorney-review memo?

5. The support team can answer whether student loans are dischargeable.

My Notes

Chapter 17

How the U.S. Legal System Works for Bankruptcy

6 to 7 hours

Learning Map

Before reading, use this map to see what the chapter helps you do in practice.

  • Explain the difference between federal law, state law, local court rules, and court orders.
  • Place bankruptcy courts inside the federal court system.
  • Understand how state law still matters inside a federal bankruptcy case.
  • Identify which source controls a practical support task.

Read the Lesson

The United States has two major court systems operating at the same time: federal courts and state courts. State courts handle most family, landlord-tenant, probate, contract, tort, criminal, and collection matters. Federal courts handle matters assigned to them by the Constitution or Congress. Bankruptcy is a federal subject, so bankruptcy cases are filed in federal bankruptcy courts, not in state court.

Bankruptcy courts are units of the federal district courts. A bankruptcy judge hears bankruptcy cases and bankruptcy-related proceedings referred by the district court. A bankruptcy ruling may be reviewed by a district court, a bankruptcy appellate panel in circuits that use one, or a court of appeals depending on the appeal path and statutory requirements. This matters for support work because a bankruptcy case may also touch a state court lawsuit, foreclosure, divorce order, tax dispute, probate issue, or business litigation.

A bankruptcy support professional needs to understand hierarchy. The Bankruptcy Code in Title 11 gives the substantive federal rules. The Federal Rules of Bankruptcy Procedure give procedural rules. Official Forms give national filing formats. Local bankruptcy rules add district-specific requirements. Judge procedures and standing orders may add courtroom-specific requirements. A case order controls the parties in that case. State law may supply property rights, lien rights, exemption rights, domestic support rules, foreclosure processes, and business entity rules.

This is why bankruptcy practice is federal but not uniform in every office. A Chapter 13 plan in one district may use a local form that looks different from another district's plan. One court may have a local negative notice procedure. Another may require a special certificate of service. One district may accept certain signatures electronically under local policy; another may require different procedures. The national course teaches the structure, but live work needs to always check the filing district.

Think of legal authority as a stack. First ask whether the issue is bankruptcy law, procedure, local filing practice, state property law, or a case-specific order. A missed local rule can be just as damaging as a missed national form. A wrong state exemption can change whether an asset is protected. A wrong county or division can create venue or notice problems. A wrong deadline can lead to dismissal, default, or loss of rights.

For paralegal and BPO teams, the practical habit is to label every task by source. If the task is a form entry, check the Official Form and instructions. If the task is a deadline, check the rule, notice, order, and local rule. If the task is an exemption, check section 522, domicile facts, state exemption statutes, and attorney instructions. If the task is a court filing, check local CM/ECF procedures and judge preferences.

Key Terms

Click a term card and explain it out loud before checking yourself.

Primary Source Focus

These are the official-law reading anchors for the chapter.

  • U.S. Courts: About U.S. Bankruptcy Courts.
  • U.S. Courts: Court Website Links for District and Bankruptcy Courts.
  • Title 11, United States Code.
  • Federal Rules of Bankruptcy Procedure.
  • Relevant local bankruptcy court rules.

Practice Scenario

Federal Case, State Property Problem

Facts: A debtor files Chapter 7 in a federal bankruptcy court. The debtor owns a home, has a state court foreclosure case, and claims a homestead exemption.

The case is federal, but several state-law facts matter. The support team can identify the filing district, foreclosure case, property county, title ownership, mortgage lien, state exemption source, domicile facts, and any sale date. The attorney decides stay effect, exemption selection, and foreclosure strategy.

Workflow: What You Do in Practice

1

Identify the federal district and bankruptcy court from the debtor's residence, business location, or assets.

2

Check the court website for local rules, local forms, standing orders, judge procedures, and CM/ECF guidance.

3

Separate national form requirements from local filing requirements.

4

Flag state-law issues such as exemptions, foreclosure, domestic support, liens, tenancy by entirety, and business authority.

Red Flags to Escalate

  • Assuming a form or plan from one district can be used unchanged in another.
  • Treating state exemptions as the same nationwide.
  • Ignoring county, division, or judge-specific procedures.
  • Using a template after a local rule or official form has changed.

Practice Assignment

Use these as workbook tasks. A trainer or attorney reviewer can grade them using the capstone rubric.

  • Pick one state and identify its bankruptcy district or districts.
  • Find that court's local rules page and list five items that could affect a paralegal or BPO task.
  • Write a one-page memo explaining why bankruptcy is federal but still affected by state law.

Knowledge Check

Choose an answer. The book gives immediate feedback and tracks your score inside this browser.

1. Bankruptcy cases are filed in which court system?

2. Which source commonly supplies property and exemption rules inside a bankruptcy case?

3. What can staff check before filing in a new district?

4. A bankruptcy court is best understood as what?

5. Who decides how state law affects a client's rights?

My Notes

Chapter 18

How the Bankruptcy Code Works in Real Practice

7 to 8 hours

Learning Map

Before reading, use this map to see what the chapter helps you do in practice.

  • Read the Bankruptcy Code as an operating system for a case.
  • Connect Code sections to forms, deadlines, documents, and workflow tasks.
  • Explain how federal bankruptcy amounts, means testing, and exemptions are updated or localized.
  • Build a source-to-task workflow for Code-driven production.

Read the Lesson

The Bankruptcy Code is not a textbook arranged in the order a case file arrives on your desk. It is a statute. Real practice requires translating statutory rules into workflow steps. Section 301 starts a voluntary case. Section 362 creates the automatic stay. Section 521 lists debtor duties. Section 541 defines estate property. Section 522 governs exemptions. Sections 501 and 502 govern claims. Section 507 sets priority. Section 523 identifies debts excepted from discharge. Chapter 7, Chapter 11, and Chapter 13 add chapter-specific rules.

A case file turns the Code into tasks. If the Code requires disclosure of property, the task becomes asset intake, value support, Schedule A/B drafting, Schedule C attorney review, and trustee-document readiness. If the Code creates the automatic stay, the task becomes urgent creditor-action intake, filing confirmation, notice routing, and stay-relief docketing. If the Code creates claim priority, the task becomes creditor classification and claims review. If the Code sets discharge exceptions, the task becomes red-flag issue spotting.

The Code also contains timing and dollar concepts that change. Certain Bankruptcy Code dollar amounts are adjusted periodically under section 104. As of the April 1, 2025 adjustment, several federal exemption and threshold amounts changed, and they apply based on filing date. A training book can teach the method, not ask learners to memorize numbers forever. Live teams needs to verify current amounts in the official source or current forms before production.

Means testing is another practical example. The means test uses debtor income, state median family income, household size, and expense standards. The U.S. Trustee Program publishes state median income data and IRS-derived standards for bankruptcy forms. These figures can vary by state and, for some expense standards, by county or locality. A support professional can know where to retrieve the data, but the attorney decides legal interpretation and unusual expense treatment.

Exemptions are where federal and state law visibly meet. Section 522 lets individual debtors exempt certain property. Some debtors may use federal exemptions, some needs to use state exemptions because their state has opted out, and some may have special domicile issues when they moved before filing. Exemption analysis may also involve homestead caps, tenancy by entirety, lien avoidance, retirement accounts, insurance, public benefits, tools of trade, wages, and local practice.

In practice, the best support teams build a Code-to-workflow map. For each issue, identify the Code section, the official form, the source documents, the local rule or court procedure, the attorney decision point, and the final work product. This makes the work teachable, auditable, and safer.

Key Terms

Click a term card and explain it out loud before checking yourself.

Primary Source Focus

These are the official-law reading anchors for the chapter.

  • Title 11, United States Code, especially sections 104, 301, 362, 501, 502, 507, 521, 522, 523, and 541.
  • Federal Register notice for April 1, 2025 Bankruptcy Code dollar adjustments.
  • U.S. Trustee Program means-testing page.
  • Official Bankruptcy Forms B106C, B122A, and B122C.
  • Bankruptcy Rule 4003 for exemption objections.

Practice Scenario

The Code Becomes a Task List

Facts: A debtor has a vehicle worth $12,000, a loan balance of $6,500, recent income above the state median, and moved from another state eight months ago.

The support team avoid deciding exemption or chapter eligibility. It can create task tracks: vehicle value and lien documents for schedules/exemption review, means-test income documents and state median lookup, domicile/move timeline for exemption law review, and attorney questions about chapter choice, exemption scheme, and any local requirements.

Workflow: What You Do in Practice

1

Create a Code-to-workflow chart for each recurring issue.

2

Verify filing-date-sensitive dollar amounts before using them.

3

Use U.S. Trustee data for means-test inputs and record the effective date used.

4

Escalate domicile, opt-out, homestead, and lien-avoidance issues.

Red Flags to Escalate

  • Using old federal exemption amounts after a statutory adjustment.
  • Using the wrong state's median income or expense data.
  • Assuming the debtor's current state automatically controls exemptions after a recent move.
  • Treating local trustee requests as optional because they are not on a national checklist.

Practice Assignment

Use these as workbook tasks. A trainer or attorney reviewer can grade them using the capstone rubric.

  • Build a Code-to-workflow map for sections 362, 522, and 541.
  • Find the current U.S. Trustee median income table and record the effective date.
  • Prepare an attorney-review note for a debtor who moved states within two years before filing.

Knowledge Check

Choose an answer. The book gives immediate feedback and tracks your score inside this browser.

1. What does a Code-to-workflow map do?

2. Which source publishes bankruptcy means-testing information?

3. Why does filing date matter for some dollar amounts?

4. What can staff do when a debtor recently moved states?

5. Who decides legal interpretation of exemptions and means-test issues?

My Notes

Chapter 19

Statewise Rules, Exemptions, and Local Practice Research

8 to 10 hours

Learning Map

Before reading, use this map to see what the chapter helps you do in practice.

  • Use a state and district map to start jurisdiction-specific bankruptcy research.
  • Identify where local rules, local forms, trustee requirements, and state exemptions enter the workflow.
  • Build a state exemption research worksheet without giving legal advice.
  • Understand why statewise bankruptcy training needs to be updated continuously.

Read the Lesson

Statewise bankruptcy learning needs to be handled carefully. Bankruptcy is federal, but local practice and state property law can change the work dramatically. A debtor in Florida, California, Texas, New York, Ohio, or North Carolina may face the same federal Chapter 7 form set, but the exemption scheme, local court procedures, trustee expectations, plan form, foreclosure process, and document customs may differ.

The first statewise question is not which exemption gives the best result. That is legal advice. The first support question is: which state, district, division, and court website control this file? From there, the team checks local rules, local forms, standing orders, judge procedures, trustee document requirements, court notices, and CM/ECF guidance. Local rules are available through the relevant bankruptcy court website or clerk's office.

The second question is exemption research. The support team can gather facts: current residence, prior residences, move dates, property location, ownership type, marital status, filing chapter, asset values, liens, and state-law indicators such as tenancy by entirety. Then the team identifies the possible exemption source for attorney review: federal exemptions, state exemptions, state opt-out law, domicile lookback, homestead limits, wildcard, motor vehicle, household goods, wages, tools of trade, retirement, insurance, public benefits, and personal injury proceeds.

A statewise exemption chart that lists dollar amounts can become dangerous if it is not maintained. State legislatures amend exemption statutes, federal amounts adjust, courts interpret laws, and local practices change. For this course, learners can use the Statewise Practice Explorer as a starting map and then verify current authority from official state statutes, the Bankruptcy Code, the court website, and attorney instructions.

In real BPO operations, statewise practice becomes an SOP library. Each jurisdiction can have a controlled profile: court website, local rules link, local forms link, Chapter 13 plan form, trustee document checklist, signature policy, hearing format, service requirements, common deficiency notices, exemption source references, state-specific red flags, and escalation contacts. Profiles can show the date last reviewed.

A learner is ready for statewise work when they can say: I know the state and federal district; I know where the local rules live; I know what exemption facts to collect; I know what not to decide; and I can write a clear attorney-review note for state-specific issues.

Key Terms

Click a term card and explain it out loud before checking yourself.

Primary Source Focus

These are the official-law reading anchors for the chapter.

  • U.S. Courts Court Website Links for district and bankruptcy courts.
  • Relevant bankruptcy court local rules and local forms.
  • 11 U.S.C. 522 and Bankruptcy Rule 4003.
  • Official state statutes for exemptions.
  • Attorney-approved state or district SOP.

Practice Scenario

Building a State Profile

Facts: A BPO team receives its first Chapter 13 matter for a debtor in the Middle District of Florida. The debtor has home equity, a vehicle, tax debt, and recent wage garnishment.

The team can create a jurisdiction profile before production: court website and local rules, Chapter 13 plan form, trustee document procedures, exemption-source checklist for attorney review, state foreclosure/garnishment facts to collect, means-test state/county inputs, and local deficiency notice examples.

Workflow: What You Do in Practice

1

Use the Statewise Practice Explorer to identify the bankruptcy district or districts.

2

Open the bankruptcy court website and locate local rules, local forms, filing requirements, and Chapter 13 resources.

3

Create an exemption research worksheet listing asset categories and state/federal source questions.

4

Record last-reviewed dates for every state or court profile.

Red Flags to Escalate

  • Copying exemption amounts from an old chart without verifying current statute.
  • Ignoring move history before deciding which exemption law may apply.
  • Missing a local Chapter 13 plan form or local service rule.
  • Using one trustee's checklist for another district without verification.

Practice Assignment

Use these as workbook tasks. A trainer or attorney reviewer can grade them using the capstone rubric.

  • Choose three states and create a one-page jurisdiction profile for each.
  • For one state, identify homestead, vehicle, wildcard, wages, tools of trade, retirement, and public benefits as research categories without choosing legal treatment.
  • Write an SOP update log showing source link, date reviewed, reviewer, and next review date.

Knowledge Check

Choose an answer. The book gives immediate feedback and tracks your score inside this browser.

1. Why can state exemption dollar charts be verified before use?

2. What is the first support question for a statewise file?

3. What can staff do with domicile and move-history questions?

4. Where are local bankruptcy rules generally found?

5. A jurisdiction profile can include a last-reviewed date.
